Deep generative models have been explored, but most rely solely on image intensity, leading to two key limitations: 1) the fidelity or trustworthiness of the generated brain images are limited, making downstream studies questionable; 2) the usage flexibility is restricted due to fixed guidance rooted in the model structure, restricting full ability to versatile application scenarios. To address these challenges, we introduce DF-DiffCom, a Kolmogorov-Arnold Networks (KAN)-enhanced diffusion model that smartly leverages deformation fields for trustworthy longitudinal brain image completion. Trained on OASIS-3, DF-DiffCom outperforms state-of-the-art methods, improving PSNR by 5.6 % and SSIM by 0.12. More importantly, its modality-agnostic nature allows smooth extension to varied MRI modalities, even to attribute maps such as brain tissue segmentation results.

title: Semi-Dense Semantic Covisibility Learning for Multi-Robot Embodied Visual Localization
authors: Wenshuai Wang, Shiyu Liu, Peifeng Jiang, Hong Liu
publish: 2025 Asian Conference on Artificial Intelligence Technology (ACAIT)
abstract: Accurate and robust visual localization is a formidable challenge for multi-robot systems due to viewpoint ambiguities, appearance changes, and dynamic occlusions. Conventional hand-crafted operators often lead to incorrect matches without domain adaptation, while existing deep learning models are characterized by suboptimal accuracy and limited generalizability. However, semantic information is considered the key to stable scene representation and remains to be fully exploited at multiple granularities. In this paper, we propose a semi-dense semantic covisibility learning framework for multi-robot embodied visual localization (SDS-VL) to enhance the efficacy of image retrieval and pose estimation by enforcing semantic consistency constraints. Specifically, the attention-driven semantic scene graph (AdSSG) is introduced for multi-view image retrieval among heterogeneous robots in a global environment, and a semantic-guided pose estimation strategy is designed to filter and prioritize matched keypoints through fine-grained scene understanding. Experimental results on 4 public datasets fully verify the remarkable effectiveness and robustness of SDS-VL in various complex scenes both indoors and outdoors, thereby providing a solid solution for embodied perception in practical applications.

title: Robust Fuzzy Clustering Based on Low-Dimensional Representation
authors: Yidi Wang, Zhenyu Ma, Xinru Zhang, Jingyu Wang
publish: 2025 Asian Conference on Artificial Intelligence Technology (ACAIT)
abstract: Clustering high-dimensional data remains a challenging task due to noise interference and redundant features. Traditional methods, such as K-Means and Fuzzy K-Means (FKM), often struggle with robustness and dimensionality issues, while subspace clustering techniques risk losing critical data information during dimensionality reduction. To address these challenges, this paper proposes a Robust Fuzzy Clustering based on Low-Dimensional Representation (RFCLDR) method. RFCLDR combines optimal mean vectors to enhance robustness while preserving essential data structures. By leveraging matrix factorization techniques, the method effectively reduces dimensionality without significant information loss. Additionally, an efficient optimization framework is developed to solve the proposed model. Experimental results on various datasets demonstrate that RFCLDR outperforms existing clustering methods in terms of accuracy and robustness, particularly in noisy and high-dimensional scenarios. The proposed approach provides a promising solution for improving clustering performance in real-world applications.

title: IPOS LLC Resonant Converter with Reconfigurable Output for 400V and 800V Architectures in EV Charging
authors: Yu Zuo, Yang Li, Qingcheng Sui, Diego Bernal Cobaleda, Bangli Du, Jiaze Kong, Xiaobing Shen, Wilmar Martinez
publish: 2026 IEEE Applied Power Electronics Conference and Exposition (APEC)
abstract: Electric vehicle charging is transitioning from 400V to 800V architectures, enabling higher power and faster charging. Off-board DC-DC converters are required to accommodate ultra-wide voltage and power ranges to ensure compatibility with both architectures. Currently, wide output voltage ranges are typically achieved through topology modifications, two-stage conversions, or hybrid modulation, with series-parallel modular operation commonly used to increase output power. Different from existing approaches, this paper introduces an input-parallel output-serial converter with a simple on-off relay switch, enabling two distinct operating modes. This design not only extends the output voltage range without requiring auxiliary circuits or complex modulation but also preserves the advantages of modular operation. Additionally, the converter provides automatic voltage balancing for the series-connected output side, while coupled inductors facilitate current sharing, even with intentional inconsistencies in resonant parameter values on the parallel input side. Furthermore, the converter’s control is simplified through pulse frequency modulation within a narrow switching frequency range, leveraging the characteristics of LLC resonant converters to regulate the output voltage for both 400V and 800V charging systems. Moreover, soft-switching is maintained across the entire voltage range. Finally, experimental results from a 1kW prototype with a 400V input and a 200-800V output validate the proposed approach, achieving a peak efficiency of 93.7%, and above 92% overall voltage range.

title: Low DC-link Voltage Operation of Three-Phase Bidirectional AC-DC Converter with Fixed Voltage Modulation Index
authors: Cheolmin Hwang, Seongwon Lee, Jung-Ik Ha
publish: 2026 IEEE Applied Power Electronics Conference and Exposition (APEC)
abstract: The operation of conventional AC-DC converters is premised on a boosted DC-link voltage to ensure an adequate voltage margin. As a result, expanding the operating range toward a lower DC-link voltage has been overlooked. A lower DC-link voltage reduces switching losses in both the AC-DC converter and the subsequent load converter, while also enhancing low-speed efficiency in variable frequency drive applications. To facilitate low DC-link voltage control, the traditional decoupled d- and q-axis current controls operate in the overmodulation (OVM) region to maximize voltage utilization. However, operating in the OVM region causes an error between the voltage reference and the converter's output voltage, which then introduces a disturbance into the current control loop. This paper proposes the one-degree-of-freedom (1-DOF) control that utilizes the voltage angle as the sole DOF, thereby improving current control performance in low DC-link voltage region. Furthermore, the paper evaluates the voltage margin in rectification and regenerative modes and proposes an appropriate DC-link voltage reference modification strategy to maximize the voltage utilization.

title: Active Power Buffer for Smoothing GPU Workload Transients in Data Center Power Delivery
authors: Lei Gu, Ahmed Nabih, Sudhir Kudva, Mostafa Mosa, Rose Abramson, Youssef Elasser, C. Thomas Gray
publish: 2026 IEEE Applied Power Electronics Conference and Exposition (APEC)
abstract: GPU workloads in data centers often produce sharp power transients, up to 1.75× the rated power, lasting several milliseconds, which strain upstream converters and grid infrastructure. To mitigate these effects, we propose an active power buffer architecture using compact bidirectional converters with high-density capacitors placed near GPUs. These buffers locally absorb or inject energy during load fluctuations, reducing stress on upstream power delivery systems. This paper presents high-frequency control schemes for the active power buffer and validates their performance in a proof-of-concept setup. Experimental results demonstrate a reduction of up to 45% in peak power drawn from the line, highlighting the potential of this approach to enhance scalability in AI-driven data center power systems.

title: Silicon Microring Resonator Gated by High Mobility Hydrogen-Doped Indium Oxide
authors: Sudipta Biswas, Saika Muntaha Bari, Alan X. Wang
abstract: We present a silicon MOSCAP microring resonator with high-mobility hydrogen-doped indium oxide, achieving <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$175 \text{pm} / \mathrm{V}$</tex> wavelength tuning efficiency, a Q-factor <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$>10000$</tex>, and sub-volt phase control with <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathrm{V}_{\pi} \mathrm{L}$</tex> of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$0.09 ~\mathrm{V} \cdot ~\text{cm}$</tex>. The device shows great potential for energy-efficient optical communication.

title: DM Conducted EMI Noise Prediction for Variable-Frequency, Variable-On-Time Interleaved Integrated TCM Totem-Pole PFC Converters
authors: Zhehui Guo, Yusheng Chen, Ripun Phukan, Misha Kumar, Peter Barbosa
publish: 2026 IEEE Applied Power Electronics Conference and Exposition (APEC)
abstract: This paper presents a numerical modeling method based on the Short-Time Fourier Transform (STFT) for predicting the differential-mode (DM) conducted electromagnetic interference (EMI) noise of two-phase interleaved integrated triangular current mode (iTCM) totem-pole PFC converters. The proposed method incorporates the time-varying switching-frequency and on-time characteristics of iTCM operation, models the drain-to-source voltages as noise sources, and uses STFT to extract their instantaneous harmonic spectra over the line cycle. The dominant harmonic contributions and frequency-domain peak envelopes are identified for both single-phase and interleaved operation, providing detailed insight into the noise-generation mechanism. DM noise transfer gains (DMTGs) are analytically derived for both single-phase and interleaved circuits. The predicted DM noise spectra are validated on a 4-kW iTCM totem-pole PFC prototype. Experimental results show close agreement with the predictions, with peak DM noise measured at 100.78 dBμV at 137 kHz for single-phase operation and reduced to 91.32 dBμV at 274.7 kHz under interleaving. The proposed STFT-based modeling framework offers an accurate and efficient tool for DM EMI noise prediction and DM filter pre-design over varying input-voltage and load operating conditions.

title: Deep Learning Framework for Multi-Class Noise Classification in Mid-Infrared Supercontinuum Generation
authors: Rakayet Rafi, Rifat Ibne Musa, M. R. Karim, M. Shah Alam
publish: 2026 22nd IEEE International Colloquium on Signal Processing &amp; Its Applications (CSPA)
abstract: Supercontinuum generation (SCG) is a nonlinear phenomenon that is highly sensitive to noise. Noise can cause spectral fluctuations that degrade coherence and stability in practical applications. While recent machine learning approaches have mainly focused on regression-based spectral prediction, classifying noise-induced degradation has received comparatively little attention. In this work, we introduce a deep learning framework for multi-class classification of noise levels in supercontinuum spectra generated in a dispersion-engineered silicon nitride $\left(\operatorname{Si}_{3} \mathrm{~N}_{4}\right)$ waveguide. A dataset of 1920 spectra is created using generalized nonlinear Schrödinger equation (GNLSE) simulations under varying pump wavelengths, pulse widths, and peak powers with controlled phase noise of different levels. Noise strength is quantified through average spectral deviation about the -40 dB spectral bandwidth and divided into five discrete classes ranging from No Noise to High Noise as per the magnitudes. The proposed hybrid architecture combines a one-dimensional convolutional neural network (1D-CNN) for spectral feature extraction with a multilayer perceptron (MLP) that ensembles the input laser parameters. The optimized model achieves an accuracy of $94.27 \%$ and a macro-averaged F 1 -score of 0.94, reliably classifying all noise levels. These results demonstrate that classification-based noise evaluation offers an interpretable and scalable approach for monitoring spectral quality in integrated broadband sources.

title: Real-Time Camera-Based Heart Rate Estimation System Using Deep Learning on Edge Device
authors: Chiquita Ahsanunnisa, Fariska Z. Ruskanda, Robithoh Annur, Rinaldi Munir, Nur Ahmadi
publish: 2026 IEEE 23rd International Symposium on Biomedical Imaging (ISBI)
abstract: Remote photoplethysmography (rPPG) provides a non-contact solution for heart rate (HR) monitoring, but deploying deep learning-based rPPG algorithms on resource-limited edge devices remains challenging. This work presents a real-time camera-based HR estimation system running on the Raspberry Pi AI Kit equipped with a Hailo-8 AI accelerator. The proposed pipeline integrates face detection, tracking, rPPG signal extraction, and HR estimation in a fully end-to-end framework. Through benchmarking on the WIDERFACE and UBFC-rPPG datasets, SCRFD-2.5G was selected for face detection and DeepPhys for rPPG extraction based on accu-racy-latency trade-offs. Quantization of DeepPhys, including a mixed-precision 20 % UINT4 and 80 % UINT8 scheme with equalization, reduced latency from <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$163,493.80 ~\text{ms}$</tex> to 28.94 ms while preserving accuracy (MAE 3.01 bpm). Additional pipeline optimizations-window chunking, batch inference, and incremental statistics-further improved real-time performance. Results demonstrate that deep learning-based rPPG systems can be efficiently deployed on edge device while meeting both accuracy and real-time requirements for practical use.

title: Comparative Analysis of PLLs Under Frequency Variations in Electrified Aircraft Power Systems
authors: S.R.Naga Praneeth, Rushiraj Jani, Rupan Sarkar
publish: 2025 IEEE International Conference on Power Electronics, Smart Grid, and Renewable Energy (PESGRE)
abstract: The development of any industry highly relies on electrification of it. Considering old oiled street lamps to rail roads and now to cars, the humankind has come leaps and bounds in electrifying things. The future of aviation industry also lies a lot in electrifying the aircraft totally or to most part of it. Additionally without compromising reliability, cost, weight and space are also to be reduced for the next generation aircraft. So a change from conventional transformer rectifier units to active front end converters are being proposed by researchers around the world. However, the change comes with challenges that include electrical hurdles like power quality issues, bi-directional power flows, advanced control strategies, efficiency and reliable contingencies. One of the addition that needs to be done to mitigate the power quality issue is to add a phase locked loop (PLL). PLLs can provide phase angle information and voltage magnitude of the utility grid. This enables the aircraft systems to function even when there are any grid disturbances (in aircraft, an engine malfunction or disturbance). This paper investigates the use of different phase locked loops (PLL) for active front end converter fitted electrified aircraft concept. Basic PLLs are investigated and detailed results are provided for different frequency variations as per aerospace standards.

title: Analyzing the Effect of Stealth Cyber Intrusions on DC Microgrid Cluster Stability Dynamics
authors: Sakshi Sharma
publish: 2026 IEEE Applied Power Electronics Conference and Exposition (APEC)
abstract: Stealth cyber intrusions in networked DC microgrid (DCMG) clusters can modify converter measurements without triggering standard alarms and, as a result, can degrade stability in ways that are not immediately visible at the supervisory level. In droop-controlled DCMGs with hierarchical control, this issue is particularly acute because the same measurement channels are used for both voltage restoration and power sharing. This work analyzes the small-signal stability of a parallel-connected DCMG cluster subjected to step-type false-data-injection (FDI) attacks on converter voltage sensors. The attack is modeled as a nonlinear, discontinuous perturbation of the voltage feedback, and a quasi-linearized state-space model is derived around the attack-dependent equilibrium. From this model, operating-point-dependent loop transfer functions are obtained and evaluated using Nyquist plots, gain and phase margins, and eigenvalue migration in the s-plane. The results show that increasing attack magnitude progressively reduces classical stability margins, drives lightly damped poles toward the imaginary axis, and ultimately produces right-half-plane eigenvalues for sufficiently large FDI levels. Time-domain simulations of the islanded DCMG confirm the predicted onset of oscillatory divergence in bus voltage and converter currents. The analysis clarifies how stealth FDI attacks on voltage measurements propagate through the hierarchical control structure and identifies critical attack levels beyond which conventional droop-based control can no longer guarantee stable operation.

title: Adaptive Reinforcement Learning for Advanced Interference Mitigation in Future Wireless Networks
authors: Saif Mohamed Baraa Alsabti, Ghassan Hamed Farhan, Aymen Mohammed Al-Dulaimi, Ali Obied, Mustafa Abdulirees Jebur, Shashi Kant Gupta
publish: 2026 International Conference on Smart Multidomain Integrated Learning Environments (ICSMILE)
abstract: The recent developments of 5G and beyond (B5G) wireless networks have placed the issue of interference management as a highly important problem, especially in dynamic channel conditions and radio-frequency (RF) hardware constraints. Nevertheless, several of the current methods of mitigating interference are either non-adaptive or not adequately so in response to real-time changes, which limits their applicability in practice. The given paper suggests a flexible hybrid system of interference mitigation, which combines traditional signal processing with deep learning (DL) and reinforcement learning (RL) to enhance interference mitigation in wireless communication networks. The implementation and test of the proposed method is performed in MATLAB/Simulink when realistic RF impairments, such as the I/Q imbalance, the phase noise, and the nonlinearity of the power-amplifier, are considered. Error Vector Magnitude (EVM), Adjacent Channel Rejection (ACR) and Bit Error Rate (BER) are used to measure system performance. The findings show that there is better interference mitigation performance and system robustness using the proposed adaptive DL-RL strategy. In the reported situations, the RL-enhanced technique minimizes BER of 0.7 at 0 dB (severe interference condition) to $1 \times 106$ at 15 dB SIR and ACR values of 0.48 at 0 dB SIR and 0.35 at 5 dB SIR are achieved under the chosen evaluation environment. Less variability in performance and greater predictability are also indicated by CDF-based analysis. These results indicate that the integration of DL and RL together with traditional methods can offer a viable and workable solution regarding the minimization of interference, throughput improvement, and dependable communication in 5G/B5G networks in the future.

title: Segmentation From Partial Views Via Patient-Specific Shape Priors
authors: Jared Vicory, Dženan Zukić, Balazs Vagvolgyi, Peter Kazanzides, Andinet Enquobahrie, Emad Boctor
publish: 2026 IEEE 23rd International Symposium on Biomedical Imaging (ISBI)
abstract: Precision surgical interventions rely on accurate integration of preoperative and intraoperative imaging to guide clinical decision-making and improve patient outcomes. Traditionally, most 3D imaging modalities capture the entirety of the target object, allowing for the segmentation of its entire volume. However, some medical imaging devices trade full field-of-view for other considerations, such as size and ability to access the target organ in unique ways. This is particularly true of ultrasound, where imaging deeper organs from outside the body is not viable due to attenuation, thus motivating the need for alternative imaging strategies. In scenarios such as image-guided intervention, it is often necessary to put this partial view of the object in its full anatomical context, such as alignment to a pre-operative MRI. We propose a method for jointly segmenting the portion of the object visible in the acquired image and estimating its full shape. We do this by combining a pre-trained shape prior with a patient-specific expectation of organ shape acquired via pre-operative imaging. In a simulated dataset for prostate MRI/US fusion, we show the ability to accurately estimate the prostate shape from ultrasound images capturing only a fraction of its total volume.

title: Performance Analysis of Cloud-Based Disaster Recovery for Workday Applications
authors: Monu Sharma
publish: 2026 International Conference on Smart Multidomain Integrated Learning Environments (ICSMILE)
abstract: The increasing reliance on cloud-native enterprise services such as Workday has intensified the need for robust disaster recovery mechanisms to ensure business continuity and service availability. As a leading provider of cloud-based Human Capital Management (HCM) and Financial Management solutions, Workday supports mission-critical business operations that require high availability, strong data integrity, and rapid recovery in the event of system failures or large-scale outages. Traditional off-site disaster recovery approaches are often constrained by high infrastructure costs, limited scalability, and prolonged recovery times, making them unsuitable for Workday’s dynamic, multi-tenant, and data-intensive cloud environment. Cloud-based disaster recovery, commonly referred to as Disaster Recovery as a Service (DRaaS), has emerged as an effective alternative by leveraging virtualization, automation, and geographically distributed cloud infrastructure. This paper presents a comprehensive analysis of cloud-based disaster recovery solutions in the context of Workday, focusing on system architecture, recovery mechanisms, and performance evaluation. A Workday-oriented disaster recovery framework is proposed that integrates automated data replication, orchestration, and continuous monitoring to achieve low Recovery Time Objective (RTO) and Recovery Point Objective (RPO) while adhering to enterprise security and availability requirements. Comparative analysis demonstrates that cloud-based disaster recovery outperforms traditional approaches in terms of cost efficiency, elastic scalability, high availability, and operational resilience. Key challenges, including data security, regulatory compliance, and performance optimization in multi-region cloud deployments, are also discussed, along with directions for future research aimed at enhancing disaster recovery capabilities in Workday’s evolving cloud ecosystem.

title: A Proximal Approach for Stain Separation and Normalization of Whole-Slide Histopathological Images
authors: Aymen Sadraoui, Astrid Laurent-Bellue, Mounir Kaaniche, Amel Benazza-Benyahia, Catherine Guettier, Jean-Christophe Pesquet
publish: 2026 IEEE 23rd International Symposium on Biomedical Imaging (ISBI)
abstract: Stain variability in histopathological images presents a major obstacle to developing accurate and reliable computer-aided diagnosis systems. In this paper, we introduce a novel stain normalization framework based on a stain separation proximal algorithm. Specifically, we first propose a new stain separation method that extracts individual stain components from a given stained image. Subsequently, we perform stain normalization by aligning the separated source stains with those of a target reference image. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed approach effectively reduces inter-image color variations while preserving the essential structural details of the tissue.

title: Crestomics: Analyzing Carotid Plaques in the Crest-2 Trial with a New Additive Classification Model
authors: Pranav Kulkarni, Brajesh K. Lal, Georges Jreij, Sai Vallamchetla, Langford Green, Jenifer Voeks, John Huston, Lloyd Edwards, George Howard, Bradley A. Maron, Thomas G. Brott, James F. Meschia, Florence X. Doo, Heng Huang
publish: 2026 IEEE 23rd International Symposium on Biomedical Imaging (ISBI)
abstract: Accurate characterization of carotid plaques is critical for stroke prevention in patients with carotid stenosis. We analyze 500 plaques from CREST-2, a multi-center clinical trial, to identify radiomics-based markers from B-mode ultrasound images linked with high-risk. We propose a new kernel-based additive model, combining coherence loss with group-sparse regularization for nonlinear classification. Group-wise additive effects of each feature group are visualized using partial dependence plots. Results indicate our method accurately and interpretably assesses plaques, revealing a strong association between plaque texture and clinical risk.

title: Speech Emotion Recognition Based on the Enhanced Initialization and Emotion-Sensitive Frequency Channel Modulation
authors: Jun Li, Chunyan Liang, Zhiguo Liu, Houbin Fan, Fengpei Ge
publish: 2025 Asian Conference on Artificial Intelligence Technology (ACAIT)
abstract: To improve the effectiveness of speech emotion recognition (SER), this study introduces a novel model termed SXAGCT-BiLSTM, which integrates a speech-adaptive Xavier initialization scheme and an emotion-sensitive frequency channel modulation mechanism. The proposed initialization method refines weight distribution by incorporating a correction factor to control variance, thereby alleviating signal amplification issues and mitigating gradient explosion, which contributes to more stable training. The emotion-sensitive modulation component, referred to as the amplified range and frequency gated channel transformation (ARFGCT), adopts a scaling strategy combined with frequency-band attention to highlight emotionally informative spectral cues, achieving fine-grained channel weighting and enhancing both classification precision and model robustness. During optimization, a joint loss function is employed for collaborative supervision, promoting intra-class cohesion while enlarging inter-class divergence, thus strengthening the discriminative representation and emotional inference capability. Experimental evaluations demonstrate that, compared with baseline approaches, the proposed SXAGCT-BiLSTM yields relative gains of 4.79% and 6.25% in weighted accuracy (WA), and 4.81% and 5.71% in unweighted accuracy (UA) on the RAVDESS and CASIA datasets, respectively.

title: A Lightweight IoT Intrusion Detection Framework with Hybrid HHO And WOA Optimization
authors: Mohd Zain Khan, Mohd Mustaqeem, Takreem Fatima Khan, Shahnwaz Afzal, Savir Ali, Md Zeyauddin
publish: 2026 Second International Conference on Emerging Computational Intelligence (ICECI)
abstract: The fast development of the Internet of Things (IoT) has made it possible to provide intelligent services in various fields, including health care, smart city, transport and industrial control. Conventional rule-based and signature-based methods of intrusion detection are not the solution anymore to solve the dynamic and intricate nature of the current IoT attacks. Machine learning-based intrusion detection systems have therefore become a feasible option as they can learn patterns using vast amounts of network traffic data and identify known as well as unknown attacks. Due to the high dimensionality of the data of IoT traffic and the presence of irrelevant features, this might reduce the performance of the detection and increase the computational expenses, which demands the need of effective feature selection. However, the majority of the current research on IoT intrusion detection is concerned with accuracy and single optimization techniques having less attention on the light weight hybrid features selection in real time implementation. In this paper, a hybrid Framework of feature selection is suggested, through the combination of HHO and WOA to improve the performance of the IoT attack detection using DW-KNN algorithm. Extensive experimental findings indicate that the hybrid model is effective in dimensionality reduction of features and high rates of accuracy. These results indicates that our proposed framework can be applied to real-time and resource-limited IoT environment.

title: Levenberg–Marquardt Trained Neural Network Based Fault Current Limitation in Synchronverter
authors: Lipun Kumar Naik, Animesh Kumar Sahoo, Mayukha Pal
publish: 2025 IEEE International Conference on Power Electronics, Smart Grid, and Renewable Energy (PESGRE)
abstract: Synchronverters are grid-forming inverters that mimic synchronous generator behavior in modern power systems. A key challenge in their control is limiting fault current during grid disturbances, which can otherwise damage equipment or destabilize the grid. Conventional virtual impedance-based fault current limitation methods often lack adaptability and real-time responsiveness under fast-changing conditions. This paper proposes a neural network-based approach to regulate inverter voltage references for effective fault current suppression. A shallow feedforward neural network is trained using the Levenberg– Marquardt algorithm to map system states to control actions with high precision and robustness. The network dynamically adjusts dq-axis voltage references in response to overcurrent conditions, keeping current levels within safe operational limits without requiring fixed parameter tuning. Simulation results show high prediction accuracy, rapid response, and strong generalization across varied operating scenarios. Compared to traditional control strategies, the proposed method offers a flexible, data-driven alternative that significantly enhances protection, adaptability, and dynamic stability in synchronverter-based systems.

title: Modeling and Analysis of Partial Discharge in High-Voltage Printed Circuit Boards
authors: Matthew J. Pompa, Andrew N. Lemmon
publish: 2026 IEEE Applied Power Electronics Conference and Exposition (APEC)
abstract: Partial discharge (PD) is a critical reliability concern in high-voltage (HV) printed circuit boards (PCBs), where insulation degradation may initiate well below breakdown voltage. Existing PCB spacing standards such as IPC-2221 are primarily intended to prevent arcing and do not explicitly address PD inception mechanisms. This paper experimentally evaluates PD inception voltage (PDIV) in uncoated HV PCBs fabricated to IPC-2221 spacing guidelines and introduces stacked and offset conductor geometries to assess geometry-dependent PD behavior. Finite-element simulations are used to compare a traditional peak-field PD criterion with a critical-area-based metric that integrates electric field magnitude over air regions exceeding a breakdown threshold. Experimental results show that stacked conductor geometries significantly reduce PDIV due to concentrated fields, while offset geometries increase PDIV without increasing nominal spacing. The results demonstrate that IPC-2221 spacing rules are conservative for PD prevention and motivate geometry-aware PD design guidelines for HV PCBs.

title: Deep Learning-Based Intrusion Detection System for Cyber-Physical Systems
authors: Khattab M Ali Alheeti, Saleem Hamad Majeed, Ahmad Muter Awaad, Abdul Kream Ah Najem, Rasha H. Sakr
publish: 2026 International Conference on Smart Multidomain Integrated Learning Environments (ICSMILE)
abstract: This “networking” of computing with physical systems, like the blending of communication at the computational layer and the processes occurring at the physical layer within Cyber-Physical Systems (CPSs) have led to increased exposure of risks for critical infrastructures. These signature-based IDSs do not have the ability to detect new and previously unknown attacks. This paper proposes a hybrid (Deep Learning-Based Intrusion Detection System) DIDS, by using RNN, LSTM, and 1D-CNN for the detection of the short-term anomalies and long-term behavioral anomalies from CPS data streams. The model utilizes temporal learning and automatic feature extraction for real-time tracking with a low false-alarm rate. A prototype of the system is evaluated using the Cyber-Physical Hardware-in-the-Loop Power System Testbed, resulting in acceptable detection performance for datainjection/falsification attacks in an MITM attack environment. The results indicate that the method has high accuracy, can be dynamically updated for newly posed risks and needs little manual updating.

title: Adaptive Multi-Scale Graph Transformer Framework for Histopathological Images
authors: Chun-I Wu, Kalyan Banda, Elizabeth M. Swisher, Heba Sailem
publish: 2026 IEEE 23rd International Symposium on Biomedical Imaging (ISBI)
abstract: Whole slide images (WSIs) contain hierarchical information from cellular to tissue architecture but their gigapixel scale poses major memory and computational challenges. Existing multi-scale graph and transformer models capture complex WSI features effectively but struggle with efficiency. We propose an Adaptive Multi-Scale Graph Transformer (AMGT) for WSI classification that addresses this limitation through two key modules: a Self-Guided Token Aggregation (SGTA) mechanism that fuses multi-resolution features to reduce redundancy, and a Prototypical Transformer (PT) that groups similar tokens into phenotype-representative prototypes with linear complexity. This design preserves essential spatial and semantic information, substantially lowering memory cost and improving interpretability by prototypical learning. AMGT achieves superior performance and efficiency, outperforming state-of-the-art models by 1.8% in predicting platinum response in high-grade ovarian cancer and 5.3% AUC in classifiying Camelyon16 datasets, respectively. These results demonstrate AMGT's capacity for scalable, interpretable multi-scale representation learning.

title: EQUI-ViT: Rotational Equivariant Vision Transformer for Robust Histopathology Analysis
authors: Fuyao Chen, Yuexi Du, Eléonore V. Lieffrig, Nicha C. Dvorne, John A. Onofrey
publish: 2026 IEEE 23rd International Symposium on Biomedical Imaging (ISBI)
abstract: Vision Transformers (ViTs) have gained rapid adoption in computational pathology for their ability to model long-range dependencies through self-attention, addressing the limitations of convolutional neural networks that excel at local pattern capture but struggle with global contextual reasoning. Recent pathology-specific foundation models have further advanced performance by leveraging large-scale pretraining. However, standard ViTs remain inherently non-equivariant to transformations such as rotations and reflections, which are ubiquitous variations in histopathology imaging. To address this limitation, we propose Equi-ViT, which integrates an equivariant convolution kernel into the patch embedding stage of a ViT architecture, imparting built-in rotational equivariance to learned representations. Equi-ViT achieves superior rotation-consistent patch embeddings and stable classification performance across image orientations. Our results on a public colorectal cancer dataset demonstrate that incorporating equivariant patch embedding enhances data efficiency and robustness, achieving an accuracy (mean ± SD) of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$86.8 \pm 0.59 \%$</tex> across rotations and outperforming standard ViT performance (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$83.1 \pm 6.93 \%$</tex>), suggesting that equivariant transformers could potentially serve as more generalizable backbones for the application of ViT in histopathology such as digital pathology foundation models. The code is available at https://github.com/fyc423/Equi-ViT.

title: Evaluation and Design of Bidirectional SSCBs Based on Half-Bridge SiC Modules for MVDC Protection
authors: Yannal Nawafleh, Daixin Chen, Baher Abu Sba’, Ahmed Ismail, Yue Zhao, Xiaoqing Song
publish: 2026 IEEE Applied Power Electronics Conference and Exposition (APEC)
abstract: This paper investigates SiC solid-state circuit breakers (SSCBs) for MVDC systems using commercially available 3.3-kV half-bridge modules. Two bidirectional topologies are proposed that parallel two half bridges and differ in metal oxide varistor (MOV) placement: (1) a line-to-line clamp between the breaker terminals and (2) a bus-referenced clamp between the DC+/DC- rails. Both topologies are analyzed in terms of operating modes, current-commutation paths, and control requirements. Copper busbars are then designed and modeled with 3D FEM, showing that the planar AC-connected MOV loop in topology 1 has about 83 nH inductance at 10kHz switching frequency, while a laminated DC busbar in topology 2 reduces this to about 5 nH at the same switching frequency. Hardware prototypes rated at 1.4 kV DC and ≈ 1.2 kA fault current validate the analysis: for the same gate resistance and MOV stack, topology 2 achieves substantially lower device voltage overshoot. Increasing gate resistance in topology 1 can reduce overshoot but significantly raises peak fault current, a critical drawback in low-inductance MVDC applications.

title: Real-Time Telemetry Modernization Using Distributed Stream Analytics for Global Product Monitoring Systems
authors: Manushi Manish Sheth
publish: 2026 3rd International Conference on Research Methodologies in Knowledge Management, Artificial Intelligence and Telecommunication Engineering (RMKMATE)
abstract: The accelerated development of Industry 4.0 and intelligent manufacturing has heightened the need to find effective real-time and predictive maintenance services in global production. This paper presents a real-time telemetry modernization system with distributed stream analytics and machine learning for the global product monitoring systems. The framework uses the Real-Time IoT-Driven Production System dataset that has <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{2, 4 6 0}$</tex> records and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{1 4}$</tex> telemetry features to create predictive models to classify maintenance and identify anomalies. The extensive preprocessing of data, feature engineering, SMOTE-ENN balancing of the classes. Three XGBoost, Multilayer Perceptron (MLP) and Isolation Forest models were applied and tested on the basis of accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, MCC, and ROC-AUC. The experiments revealed that the XGBoost model recorded the highest accuracy of 0.9878, whereas MLP model recorded the highest ROC-AUC with an accuracy of 0.9858. The baseline of the Isolation Forest had a lower performance of 0.7967 accuracy and 0.4639 ROC-AUC. The explainable Artificial Intelligence models like SHAP and LIME have been used to understand the model predictions and determine the important features that affect the maintenance decisions. The suggested framework enhances tremendously predictive precision, transparency and real-time industrial telemetry monitoring with global product monitoring systems.

title: A Reconfigurable Bidirectional Arrayed Waveguide Grating with Dual-Directional Wavelength Tuning Enabled by an Asymmetric Mach-Zehnder Interleaver
authors: Leila Vatandoust, Jae Hyeon Kim, Shashank Gupta, Taeyeon Kim, Md Munirul Islam Tusher, Hyung-Myung Moon, Sangyoon Han, Moritz Merklein, Niels Quack
abstract: We demonstrate dual-directional wavelength tuning in a reconfigurable silicon photonic arrayed waveguide grating, enabling independent red- and blue-shift control of passband center wavelengths. A bidirectional grating integrated with an asymmetric Mach-Zehnder interleaver and thermal phase shifters enables post-fabrication spectral reconfiguration and scalable wavelength control capability.

title: A Seven Level Inverter Topology with Reduced Switch Count for Electric Vehicle Applications
authors: B Hari Krishna, Dharavath Kishan, Udaykumar R Yaragatti
publish: 2025 IEEE International Conference on Power Electronics, Smart Grid, and Renewable Energy (PESGRE)
abstract: In recent years, substantial progress has been made in electric vehicle (EV) technology, necessitating the development of effective and efficient solutions in the field of power electronics. The development of multilevel inverters is the viable solution for electric vehicle power systems, with lower harmonic distortions, enhanced power quality, and the ability to transfer power in both directions. The present research is a revolutionary multisource multilevel inverter (MLI) with fewer switches. Suggested multilevel inverter utilizes two asymmetric battery sources. This distinctive arrangement facilitates the production of a more refined seven-level AC voltage output quality is improved with decreased harmonic content, addressing the rigorous power quality necessity of the electric vehicle requirement. This research provides comprehensive simulation outputs derived from MATLAB-Simulink, regarding the suggested MLI’s performance under varied operational situations and load circumstances. The simulation results involve examining the output voltage waveforms and conducting a harmonic analysis.

title: AI-Powered Hypertension Prediction: Leveraging Machine Learning for Early Detection
authors: Nidal Turab, Shahad Abu Hedeel, Batool Al Zubaidi, Suhila Abuowaida, Ahmad Aburomman
publish: 2026 1st International Conference on Emerging Technologies and Engineering Systems (ICETES)
abstract: Machine learning algorithms can enhance early diagnosis, treatment, and patient outcomes for hypertension, a significant worldwide health concern. This study classifies the presence of hypertension or not (yes, no) into classes using a Kaggle dataset that includes demographic and physical features. To assess their ability for prediction, the model integrates classifiers built using machine learning techniques such as Support Vector Machine (SVM), Naive Bayes (NB), and Random Forest (RF). The effectiveness of each classifier was evaluated using performance metrics such as area Under Curve (AUC), accuracy, F1-score, and precision. The maximum Area Under the Curve (AUC) was 98.50% for the RF method, 97.40% with NB, and 91.70% for SVM. These findings show that sophisticated machine learning algorithms may be used to accurately forecast hypertension, which will help medical professionals make well-informed decisions about patient care.

title: The Impact of Switch Commutation on Voltage Balancing in Three-Level Flying Capacitor Multilevel Converters
authors: Noah J. B. Hosein, Peter W. Lehn
publish: 2026 IEEE Applied Power Electronics Conference and Exposition (APEC)
abstract: Recent literature has showcased the flying capacitor multilevel converter as a solution for improving power density and efficiency in many power electronics applications. In practice, however, balancing the flying capacitors at their nominal voltages has proven to be a major challenge for these designs. Prior research has studied flying capacitor balancing through advanced modelling approaches, but such models typically represent the switches as ideal devices with instantaneous commutation at the switching transitions. In contrast, this work investigates how practical commutation events can affect the balancing dynamics. The analysis is performed by examining the commutation process in terms of charge transfers, which are defined by the semiconductor properties. Then, through quantitative analysis and qualitative reasoning, the impact of each charge transfer is assessed to determine if it promotes or degrades the steady-state balancing. Useful design insights are realized from the analysis, including an expression for the strength of the switch capacitance balancing effect, and an illustration of how body diode reverse recovery and asymmetric cell design can degrade balancing.
